data.title: As a developer, the interaction classifier has a fixture test validating classification accuracy
data.description:
## Description

**What**: A deterministic fixture test with synthetic epic stubs that validates the classifier produces correct severity tiers with no false positives.
**Why**: Ensures the classifier's classification logic is correct and regression-proof despite LLM variability.
**Scope**:
- IN: Two synthetic epic stubs (collision + benign overlap), expected outputs per tier, test runner integration, mock boundary specification
- OUT: Classifier implementation (S1)

## Done Definitions
- When this story is complete, a fixture test provides two synthetic epic stubs: one with a known URL namespace collision (both claim /api/users) and one benign shared dependency (both use PostgreSQL with non-overlapping schemas)
  <- Satisfies: "fixture test provides two synthetic epic stubs"
- When this story is complete, the fixture test validates that the classifier produces: (a) a conflict/ambiguity-tier signal naming /api/users, (b) a benign-tier signal for the DB dependency, and (c) no false positives
  <- Satisfies: "no false positives"
- When this story is complete, the fixture test mocks the haiku dispatch boundary and asserts on prompt construction and response parsing for deterministic results (not live LLM calls)
- When this story is complete, the test is runnable and green before the epic closes

## Considerations
- [Testing] The classifier is a haiku LLM agent — live calls would produce non-deterministic results. Mock the haiku dispatch boundary (agent input/output) and assert on the orchestrator's prompt construction and response parsing logic for deterministic testing. Follow the existing pattern used by complexity-evaluator tests

## Escalation Policy
Escalation policy: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue that could send implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. All assumptions made without escalating are documented.
